topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office smelled of lavender and old paper, a stark contrast to the echoing hallways of middle school. I clutched the strap of my messenger bag so tightly my knuckles were white. Dr. Silas Thorne, once the architect of my most miserable years—the one whose shadow loomed over every recess—now sat behind a mahogany desk, radiating a calm authority that felt entirely alien. When he looked up, his eyes, the same sharp, assessing blue I remembered, met mine. A sudden, sharp intake of breath hitched in my chest. He offered a small, practiced smile, the kind he used to deploy before delivering a particularly cutting remark. I managed a jerky nod, my gaze fixed firmly on the patterned rug beneath his polished shoes, desperately trying to anchor myself in the present moment. Every muscle in my body seemed coiled, ready to spring away if he moved too quickly.

[story 2]

I hadn't expected the silence to be so heavy. The waiting room was hushed, the ambient noise of the city filtered out by thick glass. Then he walked in. Silas. He looked… composed. The familiar sneer was absent, replaced by a measured stillness. When he approached my chair, the air thickened, pressing in on me. I felt a strange, involuntary urge to shrink, to fold myself into the seat. His voice, when he spoke, was low, modulated, devoid of any of the harsh tones I was accustomed to. "Welcome," he murmured. My throat constricted. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the action difficult. I stared at the knot of his tie, counting the threads, anything to avoid meeting those direct eyes again.

[story 3]

The first session was a blur of forced composure. I sat ramrod straight, my hands resting on my knees, trying to appear as if I were entirely in control of the situation. Silas leaned forward slightly, his expression thoughtful, almost gentle. It was too gentle. It felt like a trap sprung softly. I noticed the minute tremor in his hand as he picked up a pen, a tiny detail that sent a jolt of alarm through me. My breath caught halfway through a sentence. I wanted to bolt, to flee the confines of this room, but my feet remained rooted to the floor. I focused intensely on the pattern of the wallpaper, the floral design mocking the sudden, intense churning in my stomach. I watched him, waiting for the inevitable shift, the moment the familiar antagonism might resurface, and my skin prickled with sheer anticipation of discomfort.
